Hi Antoine,

Thanks so much for the PR. One thing I can see that may be an issue is that changes to zdesk_api.py will be lost when the API is regenerated. You can see the API generator here:

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/api_gen/api_gen.py

This works directly from the Zendesk documentation. The problem with the generator in this instance is that the documentation does not include the required parameter 'filename', nor does it include the optional parameter 'token' in the code block where the path to uploads.json is specified. My approach to dealing with this has been to create patches to the documentation. See

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/api_gen/patch.inc_exp_params

for an example. When I add extra code blocks to the documentation that do not conflict with the original declaration, then the parameters will be automatically generated. I try to relay this information upstream but in many cases I will need to maintain the patches to the documentation.

The missing body=data definitely looks like a bug in the non-generated code. Plan will be to pull the latest version of the documentation, apply all patches, make a new one for this issue, add the body=data, and put out a new version. I will shoot for having this done before the end of next week.

David, I looked into the BlueZ source and I have the same impression - I think you are running a different version of BlueZ. 

5.43 is the last official release from http://www.bluez.org/, and that's what I am using (in Arch Linux). It doesn't prefix the prompt with `[CON]` but prints out the `Connection successful` line. It looks like that behavior was changed in 2013 with [this commit](http://git.kernel.org/cgit/bluetooth/bluez.git/commit/?id=e80e4bd1ec7) - I'm guessing you are on a 2013 version of BlueZ.

Here's the function from BlueZ/gatttool that [builds the prompt](http://git.kernel.org/cgit/bluetooth/bluez.git/tree/attrib/interactive.c#n81) and here is the [connection callback function](http://git.kernel.org/cgit/bluetooth/bluez.git/tree/attrib/interactive.c#n154) (inside BlueZ) that prints `Connection successful`.

Granted, depending on the text output of a command line tool is a brittle way of doing things, but it's what we have to go on for now :). I would love to get this fork that add's a D-Bus backend updated and merged: https://github.com/daviessm/pygatt - then we could ditch gatttool.

Please let me know if you aren't able to update to a newer BlueZ for some reason - we may be able to patch in support for this older version if the prompt is the only required change.

Every fields in taskdb is used by pyspider itself, It's not appropriate to change the meaning of it or add new fields. It's better to have a mechanism for users to have their own logic of some customization fields.

Take the md5 check for example, some pages may have a clock in the page, the MD5 of hold page may change every time we crawl it. So a customised method written by user could be used to generate a more reliable md5sum with particular area of the page. And it's appropriate to be a part of user script not pyspider.

And my latest comment is an idea to implement the "tracking" mechanism. It gives a storage for the task which user script can visit and modify to store the the variables it need to track the total number of scrapes, last processed/fetch/modified time and the md5sum to check. You can get your needs without pyspider knows.

Hi, which version of python are you using? The message is raised by the underlying ssl python implementation when trying to verify the ssl certificate configuration.

Anyway you should avoid this exception modifying the ldap3/core/tls.py in your site-packages directory adding the "if" statement at line 171 and indenting the next line:
```
            else:  # code from create_default_context in the Python standard library 3.5.1, creates a ssl context with the specificd protocol version
                ssl_context = ssl.SSLContext(self.version)
                if self.ca_certs_file or self.ca_certs_path or self.ca_certs_data:
                    ssl_context.load_verify_locations(self.ca_certs_file, self.ca_certs_path, self.ca_certs_data)
```

let me know if this solves your problem.

Bye,
Giovanni

You can't use things like infinite loops with a GUI because the program gets stuck in the loop and will not update the GUI. What you need to do is put your code to read from the temperature sensor inside the `update_writing()` function, without a loop. So, where you currently have `T = temperature` will be your code to read from the sensor, and then you will set the value of the sensor and call the function again after 200ms using the code you already have.

Well, the idea in #126  was that "Max Speed in gpxinfo is not maximum speed, but average speed" and that's not the case. 

So, let's return to the idea that max speed is not correct. It is not, I agree. Probably not as falsy as trying to figure out the max speed from a graph PNG image. The problem, of course, is that there is no single best answer. The 5% algorithm is the implemented after many tests from car/mtb/hiking tracks. 

What makes you think that one arbitrary method (removing the 5% speeds) is better than another (not removing the 5% speeds)? Not removing them is definitely simpler bot it has the same problem. 

If "it's the job of a parser to take accuracy into account" then I should completely remove detection of max speed. But it's already here, and I don't plan to change the API. 

So, to recapitulate. Removing the 5% won't happen. What *can* happen is to introduce an option in:
```
def calculate_max_speed(speeds_and_distances, raw=False):
````
and
```
    def get_moving_data(self, stopped_speed_threshold=None, raw_speed=False):
```
If the caller calls these functions with `raw=True`/`raw_speed=True` he will get the max speed, but this time without any manipulation. Be aware that the result will still be falsy.

> Let me know if I am missing something.

Indeed, I didn't stress this enough. The primary speed increase comes from reducing calls into Python library and staying in pure C for as much as possible. This means switching data structures for C++ structures as needed, but the mere switch is secondary to restructuring code. What I mean by restructuring is roughly this. Compare:

![bad](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/684364/17157235/635b1602-538e-11e6-96e7-2a45165d374b.png)

Above compiles to C with lots of Py* calls, tuple construction, packing, reference counting. All in all not something you ever want in a tight loop. Sure, it could compile into a simple (?:) ternary operation, but apparently it does not. Better:

![good](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/684364/17157258/801dbfc4-538e-11e6-9336-859b4ba4d9f0.png)

A couple more Cython lines, but _far simpler_ and _much, much faster_ C++ code.

A primary goal for a solid Cython implementation is as few yellow lines as possible. Instead of profiling, [run cython with `--annotate` switch](http://docs.cython.org/en/latest/src/quickstart/cythonize.html) and inspect the resulting HTML file.

For what I know, list comprehensions don't optimize into C well, so perhaps consider replacing them. Applying only a couple such optimizations (still mostly yellow), I already achieved another 3x optimization (on top of your 5x).

I worked on a similar issue when creating the German language extension for TextBlob. Unfortunately, I do not have the time to implement this for the English version right now. @jburb if you're interested in contributing, you could have a look at http://github.com/markuskiller/textblob-de. 

I separated the ``pattern`` and ``textblob`` code base completely, making it compatible with the  original pattern library on Python2 and using a vendorised (only minimally adapted) version on Python3 (see commits between 27 July and 4 Aug 2014):
https://github.com/markuskiller/textblob-de/commits/dev?page=4
https://github.com/markuskiller/textblob-de/commits/dev?page=3  

This solution is not very efficient, as it creates a separate set of ``Tree()``, ``Sentence()``, ``Word()``, ... objects, but it was the only way I managed to retain the full range of pattern's parser options.

pycparser tracks the source line numbers for each node in the AST (`coord`).  You could use pycparser to determine the changes you want to make, figure out what line they should be on, then read in the original source file and apply the changes.

This isn't something I've done myself, but I've been considering it for one of my projects.  It will only work if you're adding or removing whole lines: `coord` also tracks column information, but because this is after preprocessing the column will be incorrect on any line that uses a macro.

I ran into the same problem, and it was the proxy not being able to loop back to the domain for the local check. My solution was to exclude such domain from the proxy:
```
export http_proxy=proxy_host:8888
export https_proxy=proxy_host:8888
export no_proxy=ox.example.com
```

With that, acme-tine will use the proxy settings to communicate with letsencrypt, but avoid using the proxy for the local domain.

I hope it helps.

Hi jeanph01!

The plugin does need to go through each table cell on initialization. The reason is that it parses and stores all of the table content within the `cache.normalized` variable; then when a sort is triggered, the parsed data is sorted directly before the entire tbody of the table is replaced with the updated content. Using this method reduces DOM interaction and speeds up the sort. This is also the reason why it is necessary to trigger the "update" method when the table contents have changed. It needs to refresh the cache.

Hi fsword73  Yes, there is!  You cna have a look at https://github.com/hughperkins/cltorch#opencl-profiling

"OpenCL Profiling

"OpenCL natively provides facilities to measure the execution time of kernels, without needing to call cltorch.finish() or similar first, using clGetEventProfilingInfo. In cltorch, you dont need to know how this works ;-) Simply call, at the start of your code:

cltorch.setProfiling(1)

"Then, after running the piece of code under scrutiny, simply call:

cltorch.dumpProfiling()

"Timings are cumulative across multiple calls to the same kernel.

"DumpTimings

"This uses the wall-clock times to measure the elapsed time in different sections of cltorch code. The way it works is, each time the cltorch c++ code calls StatefulTimer::instance()->timeCheck("some status"), the wall-clock time since the last call to ->timeCheck() will be added to the cumulative time for some status. You can pass any status as a string. Then, after running the piece of code under the scrutiny, in your Lua program, simply call cltorch.dumpTimings() to dump these cumulative timings.

"Update: please first call cltorch.setEnableTiming(true) to enable collection of timing information. This is global across all devices."

Hi, 
Yeah PIL is very specific about the types of array data it can read. A better option is to use Matplotlib's `imshow` function: 

Import it, and instruct it to display images inline in your notebook:
```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%matplotlib inline
```

Then with your array data you can preview it using `imshow` in some way similar to this:
```python
plt.imshow(X[0].reshape(width, height, 3))
```
You may or may not need to use `reshape` to change the array's shape, that depends. The `width` and `height` variables above should correspond to the width and height of the image contain in `X[0]`. 
Hope this helps.
M.

@ribeaud 
This may be the first time you're happy to find you're wrong!   PyRestTest supports submitting everything from JSON to images, etc, however admittedly doing form data this way could be made a lot easier (I'm converting this issue into a feature story to address that).

The reason for this is that PyRestTest takes a very-level approach to requests: from its point of view content type is simply a header set along with whatever supplied binary content is used for the request body.  I am currently adding some encoding awareness to the content types so that if you supply Unicode text without applying an encoding, it will set the Unicode content type and encode it.

So, for a PyRestTest body, this would be how you submit the following form data:

```python
data = {'Name': 'Gareth Wylie',
  'Age': 24,
  'Formula': 'a + b == 13%!'
}
```

**Test syntax:**
```yaml
- test:
  - name: 'POST some form data!'
  - method: 'POST'
  - body: 'Name=Gareth+Wylie&Age=24&Formula=a+%2B+b+%3D%3D+13%25%21'
  - headers: {'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'}
``

[Standard reference on this use of POST for form data those less familiar than you or I who may see it.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/POST_(HTTP)#Use_for_submitting_web_forms)

Worth noting also that you also have the escape value of [Custom Curl Options](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest#custom-http-options-special-curl-settings)  to override the way we use any of the Curl settings.  This sometimes will offer a quick way to accomplish very custom or unusual scenarios.

I've ran into the same issue and tracked the issue down to this change in [the requests library](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/18a6b601100db978f3a6e191816456e75bc47e0f/requests/models.py#L655-L671).

The reason it break betamax is that previously, a `try...except` block was used to determine wether to call `stream` or `read` on the `HTTPResponse`. Because the generated response from within betamax raised  `AttributeError` in the `stream` method, it would fail and call `read` instead.

Now, the check is done using `hasattr()` and will attempt to load the response body through `stream` instead of `read`. 

An easy way to patch is it to patch the `HTTPResponse`'s `stream` method and replace it int the `add_urllib3_response` in [`betamax.cassette.utils`](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/betamax/blob/master/betamax/cassette/util.py#L143). 

```python
# ...

class PatchedHTTPResponse(HTTPResponse):

    def stream(self, *args, **kwargs):
        return self.read(*args, **kwargs)

# ...

def add_urllib3_response(method, serialized, response, headers):
    if 'base64_string' in serialized['body']:
        body = io.BytesIO(
            base64.b64decode(serialized['body']['base64_string'].encode())
        )
    else:
        body = body_io(**serialized['body'])

    h = PatchedHTTPResponse(
        body,
        status=response.status_code,
        headers=headers,
        preload_content=False,
        original_response=MockHTTPResponse(method, headers)
    )

    response.raw = h

# ...
```

I'm not quite sure if that's the best approach to take, I'm happy to put a fix together with some feedback on what your preferred way would be to implement it. In any case, I hope it helps 😉

> Should we auto-generate some sort of doc string

I think yes. This is the case for named tuples, and it helps for libraries that use named tuples. I think the behavior should be: auto-generate a docstring if ``doc=None`` keyword is not given, otherwise just use the ``doc``. Btw, I think we could provide the possibility to add docstrings for fields:
```python
@dataclass
class Char:
    x: int
    y: int
    label: Optional[str] = field(default=None, doc='Shows name and health points')
```
(These will also help with autogeneratig docstring for ``__init__``.)

First, thanks for your interest!

At bit of history. This library started out as a PHP script around 2008 before anyone had made a PHP library for EWS. I published [this article](https://www.howtoforge.com/talking-soap-with-exchange) describing how to start, as I wanted to share my knowledge.

In the meantime, I got fed up with PHP and learned Python. I still needed an EWS library and ported my PHP code. I needed something that was fast and easy to use for calendar access which is my primary need. My company synchronizes millions of calendar items to 10.000's of accounts on 100's of servers with minimal delay. Earlier this year, looking around PyPI and answering questions on StackOverflow, I saw that there was no easy-to-use EWS library for Python and I decided to clean up my work and open-source it.

It's not difficult to create a library that consumes the WSDL and allows full access to everything. But fully auto-generating code easily becomes magic for the uninitiated, quirks are difficult to handle and consumer code becomes awkward and a mess. WSDL files are not always accessible or point to a wrong version. The library needs to "Just Work", just like Outlook "Just Works". Hence, there's a lot of tasting, guessing and workarounds for quirks and common server misconfigurations behind the scenes.

I want to provide something that is easy to use, as fast as possible and intuitive for a Python programmer. It solves the common cases, possibly sacrificing exotic functionality. I don't mind extending support beyond my own needs (I added inbox, tasks and contacts support because it was easy), but I'm also aware that a library that does everything quickly becomes a library that does nothing. Simple requirements should mean simple consumer code.

So, to answer your questions;

No, I'm not aiming to cover the full EWS functionality. If it can be done without sacrificing code readability and library usability, I'm not against it.

No, I'm not aiming to follow the Managed API. I haven't used it myself and the examples I have seen have been over-complicated for even simple needs.

I'm very welcome to contributions. New code should leave code quality in a better shape than before - faster, more "pythonic", better documented, better tested. I'm learning along the way, too.

Using a custom StorageClass fix the issue : 

```python
from storages.backends.s3boto3 import S3Boto3Storage, SpooledTemporaryFile
import os


class CustomS3Boto3Storage(S3Boto3Storage):

    def _save_content(self, obj, content, parameters):
        """
        We create a clone of the content file as when this is passed to boto3 it wrongly closes
        the file upon upload where as the storage backend expects it to still be open
        """
        # Seek our content back to the start
        content.seek(0, os.SEEK_SET)

        # Create a temporary file that will write to disk after a specified size
        content_autoclose = SpooledTemporaryFile()

        # Write our original content into our copy that will be closed by boto3
        content_autoclose.write(content.read())

        # Upload the object which will auto close the content_autoclose instance
        super(CustomS3Boto3Storage, self)._save_content(obj, content_autoclose, parameters)

        # Cleanup if this is fixed upstream our duplicate should always close
        if not content_autoclose.closed:
            content_autoclose.close()

```

If your view needs an authenticated user, decorate it with `LoginManager.login_required`. If your view can be visited by unauthenticated users, your `AnonymousUser` class should extend the `flask_login.AnonymousUserMixin` and stub out methods/properties that are throwing exceptions.

For example:

```python
from myapp import app
from flask_login import LoginManager, AnonymousUserMixin

login_manager = LoginManager()
login_manager.setup_app(current_app)

class AnonymousUser(AnonymousUserMixin):
    '''
    This is the object for an anonymous user.
    '''

    @property
    def date_format(self):
        return 'YYYY-MM-DD'

    @property
    def has_premium_features(self):
        return False

login_manager.anonymous_user = AnonymousUser
```

Unfortunately, the `python_http_client` library obfuscates error messages from the server by wrapping  `HTTPError`s from urllib in its own custom Exception class.  This is a dependency of the sendgrid python library and is out of the scope of this library.

Most likely, your request to the sendgrid API is invalid in some way.  Make sure that the data in your mail object complies with the following specifications from sendgrid's web api documentation https://sendgrid.com/docs/API_Reference/Web_API_v3/Mail/index.html.  If you need to dig in further, I would use some sort of a debugger (I like pudb) to step through the code until you get to urllib's exception.  Then, you should be able to read the message back from the server when you inspect the Exception.  In my experience, you should get enough information from that to solve your issue from there.

I've thought of a better way to solve this. It is very much a hack, and I still think flask-restplus should fix their extension so that it does not break native flask features, but it should get you up and going safer then how you have it handled above.

It looks like the errorhandler method for restplus uses the same signature that flask error handler does, so you could take advantage of duck typing and access this internal method to set the errors on the restplus level: https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/jwt_manager.py#L81

```python
from flask import Flask
from flask_jwt_extended import JWTManager
from flask_restplus import Api

app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['JWT_SECRET_KEY'] = 'super-secret'  # Change this!
jwt = JWTManager(app)
api = Api()

# This is where the duck typing magic comes in
jwt._set_error_handler_callbacks(api)
```

This would obviously be prone to break if I changed how the underlying part of this extension worked, as you are accessing a private method that doesn't have any guarantees on it, but I do not see any reason why that method would change in the foreseeable future, and this would insure that any new or changed error handles in this extension would get properly set on the flask-restplus extension.

Hope this helps :)

Ok, I think I understand a bit better how that autoescape feature works in jinja.

The [auto-escape overrides](http://jinja.pocoo.org/docs/2.9/templates/#autoescape-overrides) was handled by an extension in jinja 2.4, and became a builtin in jinja 2.9. However the autoescape mecanism was already functional in 2.4, with no extension. Only the autoescape overrides needed an extension.

The problem with your last patch is that you check the autoescape function exists for XML, but you create the template from a string, not a file. That means jinja will never check the filetypes marked for escaping. It will instead pass 'None' to the autoescape function, which returns the value of the 'default_for_string' parameter of jinja2.select_autoescape function since 2.9. If the user didn't request auto escaping for strings, the rendering will fail.

So if you want to respect the escaping values set in the environment, you're right, using file-like objects and calling get_template instead of from_string is an option. Jinja would call the autoescape function and guess its corresponding filename, reflecting the user environment. As a result, you wouldn't need to rely on the autoescape overrides mecanism.

The only custom one I wrote is this one, I'm including it here just to be on the safe side:

```
class CanonicalDomainMiddleware(object):
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response
        if settings.DEBUG or not SITE_DOMAIN:
            raise MiddlewareNotUsed
        # One-time configuration and initialization.

    def __call__(self, request):
        """If the request domain is not the canonical domain, redirect."""
        hostname = request.get_host().split(":", 1)[0]


        # Don't perform redirection for testing or local development.
        if hostname in ("testserver", "localhost", "127.0.0.1"):
            response = self.get_response(request)
            return response
        # Check against the site domain.
        canonical_hostname = SITE_DOMAIN.split(":", 1)[0]

        if hostname != canonical_hostname or request.META.get("HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO", "") != 'https':
            if request.is_secure():
                canonical_url = "https://"
            else:
                canonical_url = "https://"
            canonical_url += SITE_DOMAIN + request.get_full_path()

            return redirect(canonical_url, permanent=True)

        # Code to be executed for each request before
        # the view (and later middleware) are called.

        response = self.get_response(request)
        # Code to be executed for each request/response after
        # the view is called.

        return response
```

A dot in column 18 means that it is not relevant. If the sample assembles into a contig that has no differences from the reference sequence, then you will see dots in columns 14-30 because they relate to variants. Since there are no variants to report, dots are put in those columns This is what happened in row 7.

Some of the reference datasets (eg CARD) have information about variants in addition to just having sequences. ARIBA reports whether or not those known variants are found. Column 14 indicates whether the variant is known (1) or not known (0). Column 18 says whether or not the sample has the known variant. So column 14=1 and column 18=0 means that this is a known variant, but the sample does not have it.

If the row is reporting a novel variant, then both column 14 and column 18 are set to zero. This is what is being reported in row 10.

Hope that all makes sense!

This is expected, but not documented. `sops` uses the file extension to figure out which encryption/decryption method to use. `yaml` and `json` treat the files as data trees, whereas everything else is treated as a binary blob.

You encrypted the file with a `json` extension, which treated it as a data tree, but attempted to decrypt with a `enc` extension, or binary blob, which failed because the expected format wasn't present.

If you want to change the extension of a file without changing its type, you need to use `--input-type` and `--output-type`.

Taskcluster builds have a less deterministic `job_type_name` of 'build-<platform>/<build_type>'. From the log I see this is an example of using Package Reloader (not really an end-user flow), probably with two Sublime windows open. There are known problems with LSP sharing language server state between multiple windows during startup - the first KeyError seen is already tracked as https://github.com/tomv564/LSP/issues/253.

It's likely that didOpen for one window was sent to the wrong language server at startup, then the subsequent hover for that window fails.

I recommend closing this issue as few people will reproduce it, especially when the LSP behaviour is fixed.

I found the issue - printToCLI wipes out the escapes. The easiest solution seems to be adding a test for `isinstance(value, six.text_type)` (python version-agnostic unicode string check) and simply printing the unicode string rather than always running `print(u("{0}".format(value)))`.

@emmett9001 can I get your opinion on the version number? I decided to go with Kafka's version number followed by our own counter.  Anyone else is welcome to comment as well, of course.  It's just a scheme that made sense to me, no particular attachment there.

Kafka's semantics guarantee that each message will be consumed at least once - it provides a mechanism (offset tracking) that ensures this. Kafka also has no facilities that allow at-most-once delivery across a consumer group, though we could certainly add example documentation on how to store the additional information necessary to build it oneself. We haven't given any thought to how users can subclass the main classes of pykafka - I suppose the reasoning for this is that they're simple enough that we hope clients won't have to. I agree that having `_rebalance` call `commit_offsets` even when `auto_commit_enabled=False` is an ugly wrinkle, and I'd like to fix it in the future if possible. It sounds like, beyond the ugliness, it's not actually breaking anything for you.

I'm not certain, but it does seem like the existing pre-rebalance callback will satisfy your use case here. If all you need is a hook that will let you call `flush_status` on the set of partitions held by the consumer prior to the rebalance, you can do that in [this callback](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/9550e910ad98b1c3b5ba35a637123141fe474bbb/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L554). Though it's called `post_rebalance_callback`, it's actually called before the rebalance is finalized (when `self.cns` is reassigned). In this callback, you can get the currently owned set of partitions and their current offsets (that is, the set owned prior to the completion of the rebalance). As far as I can tell from your example, this is the perfect time to call `flush_status`.

You may also want to take a look at https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/288 and see the use case that @ryan-stateless was dealing with that prompted us to add this callback in the first place. It's the most similar use case to yours that I've come across.

The way to support shoehorn and structlog is via the callable attributes pattern that already exists:

```python
def extract_suff_from_record(record):
    return {...}

with LogCapture(attributes=extract_stuff_from_record):
    ...
```
I don't think `LogCapture` can reasonably do more as it's a helper for standard library logging, and other frameworks that plug in to that, like shoehorn, need to find ways to store their extra context on a normal logging `Record` such that only a callable can sensibly extract them.

The entire point here is to make sure the choice of edge padding doesn't affect the result for values/structures near the edges. For binary, this means (paradoxically) we have to erode into "on" pixels. Otherwise, if we padded with zeros, the invisible, padded pixels would effectively away structures near the edges during the operation. This behavior is unexpected and bad.

For grayscale erosion, which sets the pixel to the lowest value in the neighborhood, the edges need to be sufficiently higher than the neighborhood to avoid a similar problem. `reflect` seems to work for this. It shouldn't need to be changed.

Patroni creates replication slots on the node where the replica wants to stream from (usually master). AFAIK, starting from 9.6 postgres will protect wals which were not consumed from replication slots from clean up, but unfortunately you are on 9.4.

If you don't have WAL archiving enabled, you can try to play with pg_receivexlog, but you have to stop it as soon as pg_basebackup is done. Also don't forget to move received wals into pg_xlog.

BTS, it's always good to have continuous archiving enabled, because people tend to do a lot of stupid things, like dropping all tables on production db :)

Incrementing the timeline is an important safety feature. Also it does not cause any problems - you're not going to run out of timeline ID's.

A small detail, when given a recovery.conf on a properly shut down instance PostgreSQL does not assume a crash, it knows from the control file that the server was properly shut down. It just starts recovery mode to start replicating. The timeline gets incremented on promotion, as it should.

I' not sure what failure mode you are proposing here. Having both masters and standbys archive WAL makes no sense. WAL archive when being used a source for recovery is intended to be a single resource shared by all nodes in the cluster. This archive will contain all the timeline splits and WAL generated by any succession of masters in the cluster. If the succession of masters goes offline and a lagging standby on an old timeline determines it is allowed to promote (maximum_lag_on_failover is not exceeded), it will reuse the timeline identifiers. The timeline history file and following WAL files will overwrite the existing ones in the archive. If the old servers now come back they will notice  different timeline split from what they have locally and will refuse to follow the master. Under most, but not all circumstances Patroni is smart enough to automatically rewind them.

The default server of connexion is the werkzeug server which is used by flask. So if you want to provide an https server there are several ways to go. One way is described by flask [here](http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/111/) and looks like this:

```python
from OpenSSL import SSL
context = SSL.Context(SSL.SSLv23_METHOD)
context.use_privatekey_file('yourserver.key')
context.use_certificate_file('yourserver.crt')

app.run(host='127.0.0.1',port='12344', 
        debug = False/True, ssl_context=context)
```

But there you will encounter the problem that connexion doesn't provide a `ssl_context` parameter at the moment. This is because flask doesn't either, but it uses `**kwargs` to send the parameters to the underlying werkzeug server.

I'm currently working on a pull-request to provide **kwargs options for werkzeug through connexion. Check it out here: #72

@adrienpacifico De plus, toute branche `master` d'un dépôt doit fonctionner avec la branche `master` de tous les dépôts.

Cela garantit que si tu fais `git pull` sur tous les dépôts chacun sur la branche `master` cela fonctionne.

Sinon c'est une erreur de notre part. Pour y remédier on peut en effet dire qu'on "revert" les commits malheureux immédiatement.

Face aux changements de Core et France, même si ceux-ci restent cohérents entre eux, ton code lui peut casser. C'est pour cela qu'on peut mettre en place le "semantic versionning" où on indique via le numéro de version majeur/mineur si un changement est susceptible de casser le code appelant. Mais il faut réinstaller le paquet avec `pip` après chaque `git pull` (d'où le script [`update.sh`](https://github.com/sgmap/openfisca/blob/master/update.sh) j'imagine).

Ou alors on dit que tant qu'on utilise `git` on est en mode développement "à la pointe du progrès" et si on veut plus de stabilité alors on utilise OpenFisca via `pip install`, et on exprime dans le code utilisateur une dépendance avec numéro de version du paquet `pip`.

A segment represents a full request/response cycle and all the local work done will be recorded as subsegments and attached to this segment. SegmentNotFound means the SDK cannot correlate the S3 upload to a request for your Django app.

The library django-storages you are using utilize boto3's S3 multi-part upload to increase throughput. And this multi-part upload functionality uses a thread pool. You can see the code here. https://github.com/boto/s3transfer/blob/develop/s3transfer/manager.py

This is a high-level API which internally uses multiple threads to upload to S3. When it creates a new thread and use that thread to make a call to S3, the SDK is still trying to create a subsegment which records useful information of this downstream call, but it lost the context due to the new thread and the empty thread local storage. 

In order to work with S3 transfer manager, this manager also has to be patched so that whenever it creates a new thread it injects thread local storage to the new thread. More general, the SDK today is incompatible with any library that manages its own thread pool.

For the RWP dealiasing I wrote a one off script which created an empty Radar object using `pyart.testing.make_empty_ppi_radar` and added the velocity data.  The script had a number of hard coded values to get things working but it got the job done.  I can share the full script if you would like but I don't think it would be of much use.  

The key snippet which I would only use to get something working quickly is:

``` Python
radar = pyart.testing.make_empty_ppi_radar(75, 800, 1)
radar.fields['velocity'] = {'data': vel}
radar.fields['snr'] = {'data': snr}

nyquist_vel = dset.variables['vband'][0] / 2.
gatefilter = pyart.correct.GateFilter(radar)
gatefilter.exclude_below('snr', -10)
gatefilter.exclude_invalid('snr')

corr_vel = pyart.correct.dealias_region_based(
    radar, vel_field='velocity', keep_original=True,
    gatefilter=gatefilter, skip_along_ray=10, rays_wrap_around=False,
    nyquist_vel=nyquist_vel)
radar.add_field('corrected_mean_doppler_velocity', corr_vel)
```

@narsariamanu Thanks for the report!  The `400 InvalidArgument` response from the API indicates that the `hour.6` query itself is not allowed.  The integer key needs to be escaped, e.g.:

```python
db.collection(u'validHours').where(u'hour.`6`', u'==', True).get()
```

There is a [`FieldPath` helper](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/8fa7ab2f9071bc41e610c0ab4f0319f8770d0ea9/firestore/google/cloud/firestore_v1beta1/_helpers.py#L113) which knows how do to that escaping, e.g.:

```python
from google.cloud.firestore_v1beta1._helpers import FieldPath

path = FieldPath.from_string(u'hour6')
escaped_path = path.to_api_repr()

db.collection(u'validHours').where(escaped_path, u'==', True).get()
```

Hi @mmckerns 

I have the same issue with `ProcessingPool.map()` from `pathos.multiprocessing`. Here is a minimal example of the issue:

```
from pathos.multiprocessing import ProcessingPool as Pool
import time

def worker(k):
    time.sleep(10)
    return k

p = Pool(4)
p.map(worker, range(100))
```
When pressing `<Ctrl>+C` while this is running, I get a long traceback, and multiple `KeyboardInterrupt` are shown in the console, but the terminal hangs without any possibility to close. Pressing `<Ctrl>+C` again leads to more output, but the program is not closed.

It seems to me that the worker-processes are closed upon a `KeyboardInterrupt`, but new processes are being spawned.

I am using Python 2.7.12 and Pathos 0.2.1.
Thanks for your gist, it's a nice collections of tests.  If you think it's a pickling issue, then the thing to do is to check if `dill` can serialize the objects -- see `dill.check` and `dill.copy`.

@mgserafi: I can't reproduce the error you are seeing.  What is your OS and version of `multiprocess`?   If you are on Windows, it could be due to a lack of a C++ compiler.  I'm using the current development version of `multiprocess` and a Mac.

Hey, alright…no problem.  I'm just glad we solved it, and it's not something nasty lurking within `multiprocessing`.   So, your case is one of the reasons I have the `pathos.pools` do what they do.  I was bitten by the same thing when I first started using `Pool` -- it's a common problem.
Thank you

I ran a contrastive benchmark using a representative transformer model on a P3 instance using a rather small input corpus (n=500). Bottom line: caching this doesn't help with small beam sizes, the workload of the linear projections is just too small to be a bottleneck for the P3. For larger batch sizes however, a small improvement can be observed:

| beam_size 1    | sec/sent  |
| ------------- |:-------------:|
| master        | 0.2533 |
| this PR       | 0.2707 | 

| beam_size 8   | sec/sent  |
| ------------- |:-------------:|
| master        | 0.0760 |
| this PR       | 0.0747 | 

| beam_size 16    | sec/sent  |
| ------------- |:-------------:|
| master        |  0.0544 |
| this PR       | 0.0521 | 

| beam_size 32    | sec/sent  |
| ------------- |:-------------:|
| master        | 0.0430 |
| this PR       | 0.0411 | 

On the CPU a speed up even for batch size 1 can be observed. I'd be curious to see how it behaves on a p2.

        [ "ec2.regions.id.vpcs.id.security_groups.id.rules.id.protocols.id", "equal", "TCP" ],
    ]
}

Well this fixes the problem, but I just realized that the top-level 'actions' key in actions.yaml is not actually required (or expected).

Ignoring the error pushes the problems further down the track. Now the service being managed by the charm may fail since it will attempt to start bound to a port already in use. Now the charm can later close the port, breaking the co-hosted units which think the port is open.

I think this problem would be better handled by making open_port return a more informative error message, so the charm is able to handle the issue as it chooses. Most charms will not be able to handle port conflicts, so it is better if they fail sooner. In nrpe's case, it can ignore an open-port already-open failure, assume that any other units dealing with port 5666 are also nrpe units (and not, say, Apache with weird config), and that charms are never removed once deployed and the close-port races can be ignored.

My point about latency is that you may be serving both traditional-web-application and websocket/autobahn interfaces for the same application.  I understand that you could do this a different way, but keeping all your services the same can really simplify deployment pipelines, and it's definitely something that Twisted works hard to make possible, and Autobahn specifically allows for.

But, as @Lukasa explained - there's no overhead beyond the initial TLS handshake for websocket connections anyway, since `wss://` clients won't advertise an ability to speak HTTP/2.

I agree with @hawkowl that all WAMP should care about is getting the event there. What happens inside the user code is not our concern, and we shouldn't go there. Acknowledgement that components have successfully executed code based on a received subscription are an application-level concern. This will often involve more than just the subscription handler executing without an error, and components other than the publisher may be the ones concerned with the outcome.

Yes, this is a known issue, unfortunately.  The problem is that you're specifying 5-grams for your CNN filters, but you have no word that is over 4 characters, so the CNN filter crashes.  Detecting this and fixing it on our side is actually quite hard, but it's relatively easy to fix in your configuration.  Either you can change the size of the filters in your CNN, or add padding around the characters so that you'll always have at least five characters (I'd recommend the second option).  You can see how we do that with BiDAF here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/83f0c5ecaa1020371d7788f6683764ff806dbe36/training_config/bidaf.json#L9-L16.  You could just do `"end_tokens": [0, 0, 0, 0]`, making sure there's always enough 0 padding, or you could put explicit begin and end markers, like the BiDAF model does.
We're going to wait for the dependencies to settle down before we address this.

Great! I am pleased and impressed that you have made anisotropic feature-finding a first-class citizen in all these parts of the feature-finding code.

I'm not yet convinced that we should be using the raw image to perform characterization. I think the important mass is the mass of the image that the algorithm is actually using. I think of the processed image as closer to the "truth" than the unprocessed one. In particular, the long-wavelength variations due to uneven lighting are a systematic error and we should be removing them. Let's discuss this further.

We currently use `raw_image` in just one place: to compute the noise. This is actually problematic, because if users want to perform their own preprocessing, `locate` does not let them pass in the original image. Perhaps `locate` should optionally accept two images, raw and processed. If we do ultimately use `raw_image` in more places, this definitely should be addressed.

@ataffanel I would encourage you to think about this a little more.  There are a few things that are changed here:

# exit(main())
It is a good practice to wrap main in exit like: exit(main())

why? well if you ever want to test the main method for your python code, using sys.exit is just not going to work.  It is good to get in a habit of doing this.

# DRY and variable naming
Because the code was not DRY, there was a lot of copy and pasting going on, for instance:

Might as well make some notes for that documentation here I guess.  With regard to: https://github.com/gogetdata/ggd-recipes/blob/38822e9a4963b54fc359be7a3b04c110d53b6bd9/recipes/Canis_familiaris/canFam3/canfam3-dbsnp/pre-link.sh

* Don't target for bash. Target for generic/posix /bin/sh.  Make use of https://www.shellcheck.net/ with `#!/bin/sh` as the shebang.
* Make sure the scope of the script, any side effects, are confined to the target $PREFIX. Conda post-link shouldn't be used as configuration management, and post-link shouldn't have side effects outside of the target environment or system-wide. I don't think this specific example has an issue with that.
* Any commands invoked within the script that are not shell built-ins should be provided as run requirements. This script has `gsort`, for example, but makes the assumption that `wget` is universally available, which it really shouldn't.

Hi Jake, for future reference, this is more of a conda-recipes or
conda-build issue.

Setting CMAKE_OSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET to "" is fine, but the implication is
that you no longer explicitly know what your OSX deployment target is (it
is variable based on what computer you use to build the recipe).  In order
to get older or newer deployment targets, you sometimes have to have old
(or newer) SDKs/XCode versions, and those are hard to come by.

My advice would be: proceed as you suggest, and if you have compatibility
issues coming from consumers of your built package(s), then explore
alternative build platforms (Travis CI with Mac support, or perhaps
anaconda.org with a mac worker.)  We keep old OS X machines around for this
exact reason.

Our case is for ES in a non-logstash config.  We're dumping a couple billion documents per hour to highly optimized indexes.   We did some extensive performance testing last summer w/ ES 1.3.x and found that after a few hours ES performance would begin to degrade quickly.  After a consultation w/ ES support, we tweaked the blocks.write and codec.bloom.load, ES was able to keep up again.   I think we peaked out around 700k dps.

If you read [this comment](https://github.com/elastic/curator/issues/685#issuecomment-231424273) in #685, you'll see that it's not a regression.  Curator 4 _depends_ on the `_cluster/state` endpoint, and AWS ES does not provide it.

When I wrote Curator 4, which is a major change over Curator 3:

1. Due to new APIs, Curator 4 was not going to be reverse compatible with pre 2.0 versions of Elasticsearch.
2. AWS ES was tied to ES version 1.5
3. I hoped that if they ever released a newer version it would support the `_cluster/state` endpoint.

AWS ES now supports a newer version of Elasticsearch, but it still doesn't support the `_cluster/state` endpoint.

Why does Curator 4 depend on the `_cluster/state` endpoint, when Curator 3 did not?  Because Curator 4 extends a lot of functionality that Curator 3 did not have, like the ability to filter by age with the index `creation_date`.  The method in question is [`IndexList._get_metadata`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/indexlist.py#L136).  Other code that depends on the cluster state includes the `master_only` testing and checking whether indices are open or closed. A workaround to not use cluster state would mean having to complicate the code, considerably, and it would result in a fragmented user experience, as it would mean that some users simply couldn't use some functionality.  I got into that in Curator 3, and it was a pain to maintain and document, and I was constantly having to explain that feature X was not supported in use case Y.  I want to keep the experience uniform, so I am using the simplest way forward, which is to use `_cluster/state`.

As mentioned in that linked comment, the best case scenario for you would be to use Curator 3 still, as it supports versions of ES up through 2.x.  I also suggest asking AWS ES to change the policy of disallowing the `_cluster/state` endpoint, since it was their decision to remove that.  Perhaps they could be persuaded to reverse that decision.

I'm not familiar with the FFT code so I'm not sure about #185, but it looks like this PR breaks the CI tests (as you can see by clicking through to Travis CI above). You can run the tests locally by running `nosetests tests` in the git root directory.

The tests are currently broken just because you used `x.conjugate()` where you probably should have used `anp.conj(x)` (it looks like the name `anp.conjugate` isn't wrapped right now, but they're the same function). I made that change locally and now the tests fail numerically. I suspect approx_fprime follows some different convention (@dougalm has explained the complex differentiation conventions in some other GitHub issues), but our convention is pretty well specified (namely: agree with numerical derivatives) and is encoded in the tests.

I suggest taking a look at the tests in tests/test_fft.py and seeing whether there's some case that isn't covered, or if you can write a failing test against numerical derivatives.

* a pull request adding the `Link` attribute and tests
* a pull request adding support for `Link` attributes to the attribute factory and tests
* a pull request adding support for `Link` attributes to the Pie object set and tests
* a pull request adding X.509 certificate code to the Cryptography engine and tests
* a pull request adding support for `Link` attributes to the server engine and tests

@pganssle - Can you describe your solution in more detail?  Note that typically tzinfo objects are shared between datetime instances.  Even if you don't implement them as singletons, whenever you add a timedelta to a datetime instance, you get two datetimes with the same tzinfo.  Now, if you call fromutc() on one of those instances would both get the _fold property?

The only workable solution that I can think of would be to maintain a list (maybe in a weakref.WeakKeyDictionary) of datetime instances that in PEP 495 world would have fold=1.  The fromutc()  can then add to this list and utcoffset consult this list in the ambiguous cases.

There are two changes glued together in a single commit: moving of some http methods to B2RawApi and a change in how Bucket objects are constructed.

The second change forces the user to explicitly provide an instance of raw_api and account_info when constructing the bucket object. It is a non-small change in the API interface (thank you for keeping it on a branch for discussion).

It used to be
```py
api = B2Api()
bucket_dict = api.something('...')
bucket = BucketFactory.from_api_bucket_dict(api, bucket_dict)
```
and now it will be
```py
api = B2Api()
bucket_dict = api.something('...')
raw_api = B2RawApi()
account_info = StoredAccountInfo()  # wrong! should be account_info = B2Api().account_info
bucket = BucketFactory.from_api_bucket_dict(raw_api, account_info, bucket_dict)
```

The change also isolates the Bucket class from the B2Api class completely, which might be fine, but we might want to use some B2Api methods from something like `Bucket.sync_down()` (for example `B2Api.download_by_id()`). Passing a B2Api instance encapsulated its properties like `account_info`, `raw_api`, `cache` or any other (maybe `b2_session` in the future), which might just be useful when performing all kinds of bucket operations. Therefore I think we should not make this change and keep Bucket having an `api` as a member. At the same time, it is ok to do something like `self.raw_api = api.raw_api` in `Bucket.__init__` for convenience.

As for moving the actual http calls from Bucket to B2RawApi, I wholeheartedly approve.

This is to address #1098

Current way:
```python
objective = Objective(layer, categorical_crossentropy)
target_var = objective.target_var
train_loss = objective.get_loss()
val_loss = objective.get_loss(deterministic=True)
```
Vanilla way:
```python
target = T.matrix()
train_loss = categorical_crossentropy(get_output(layer), target).mean()
val_loss = categorical_crossentropy(get_output(layer, deterministic=True), target).mean()
```
Another possibility (1):
```python
def make_target_var(output_layer, name='target', dtype=theano.config.floatX):
    ndim = len(output_layer.output_shape)
    return return T.TensorType(dtype, [False] * ndim)(name)
target = make_target_var(layer)
train_loss = categorical_crossentropy(get_output(layer), target).mean()
val_loss = categorical_crossentropy(get_output(layer, deterministic=True), target).mean()
```
Another possibility (2):
```python
def make_var_like(var, name=None):
    return var.type(name)
output = get_output(layer)
target = make_var_like(output, name='target')
train_loss = categorical_crossentropy(output, target).mean()
val_loss = categorical_crossentropy(get_output(layer, deterministic=True), target).mean()
```
Another possibility (3):
```python
def construct_loss(loss_function, output_layer, target_var=None, **kwargs):
    output = get_output(output_layer, **kwargs)
    if target_var is None:
        target_var = output.type(name='target')
        return loss_function(output, target_var), target_var
    else:
        return loss_function(output, target_var)
train_loss, target = construct_loss(categorical_crossentropy, layer).mean()
val_loss = construct_loss(categorical_crossentropy, layer, target, deterministic=True).mean()
```

All of the `.mean()` could be replaced by the `aggregate` function drafted above, but that's mostly useful if you do more complex things than taking the mean.

Looking at the different variants, I'm now pretty convinced that the one thing we should not hide is the `get_output` call. Doing so will trick people into introducing multiple `get_output` calls into their code without realizing, which will potentially run into #104.
And looking at the vanilla variant, I'm pretty convinced the `Objective` class does not only make it more opaque, but also makes it look like creating a loss expression was a complex task.

Would you be fine with just having a helper function that avoids the `T.matrix()` (or `T.tensor3()`, or `T.vector()`) call, as in variant (1) or (2)? For more symmetry to the `InputLayer`, we could also have a `TargetLayer` that you can put on top of every output layer of your network to create the variables for you, but nothing more. That wouldn't be a lot different to variant (1), though.

If we go with this, the docs will need fixing.  "pip install Lasagne" will have to be replaced with "pip install -r https://github.com/.../1.0/requirements.txt" and then "pip install Lasagne".  So I'd suggest making a separate pull request for this that also updates the docs.

There's a small trade off there: you can't tell users to "just use pip install" anymore.  But I think that's okay.

It may also trip up some people that use a Git checkout of Lasagne.  If the requirements.txt updates frequently as you do a git pull, I imagine it might happen that you forget to re-run the `pip install -r requirements.txt` to get the latest required Theano.  Maybe worthwhile mentioning that in the docs.

My input is that a symbol without an address is a concept that CLE handles using the idea of an "import symbol", which comes from ELF binaries. These symbols are given an address of zero by the binary format itself, and cannot be used for anything other than marking a yet-unresolved dependency. This seems like the exact use case you're working with currently, and my recommendation would be to refactor the symbol binding process to use import and export symbols like the rest of CLE, though I don't know anything about mach-o binaries and don't know how well this would work. PE files (windows) don't have an explicit concept of import/export symbols, but we added them in our implementation of the PE loader to simplify the data model.

IRIs vs. URLs is a red herring here.  *reserved* characters in a URL are still reserved in an IRI; the decoding that happens is all reversible and idempotent.

The issue here is one about whether you are manipulating the text of the URL itself (which is what URL has always been; twisted.python.url just had a bug where it would let you put in some invalid characters), or whether you're manipulating a value *stored in* a URL, which is not visible as part of the URL.  In other words: does `%` mean `%`, or does it mean "escape sequence beginning, please hold"?  One of `URL`'s invariants is that everything can round trip: this would not be true if everything were decoded, since `/%` would turn into `/%25`, or an error.

A quick sketch a two-type proposal:

`DecodedURL(path="%&")` -> `DecodedURL(URL(path='%25%26'))`
`DecodedURL(URL(path='%25%26')).path = `%&`

I think I actually like this, perhaps with the caveat that this could be a `.decoded` or `.unquoted` attribute on `URL` itself, so we don't need to import an extra wrapper name.

I strongly *dislike* exposing a bunch of public APIs for encoding and decoding, since it's easy to screw that up, and you end up passing around context-free strings all the time, ending in the inevitable concatenation of some unquoted HTML, some quoted URL, some unquoted SQL, and etc.

I'm not keen on sacrificing the multiple advantages that preferring a self-contained hotdoc configuration file offers to cater for the perceived ineptness of maintainers. Yes, it's true, if you don't pay attention to your software, chances are it will rot, and yes we should make our best to leave the less margin for error, *as long as this doesn't get in the way*.

I've been working on the autotools integration for that reason specifically, and I do believe the offered interface is now sufficiently user-friendly, requiring that a source list be updated when a project using autotools makes use of hotdoc is not such a price to pay compared to the advantages it offers.

I do agree that it is very unfortunate that the list of sources cannot be shared, but this is a limitation of autotools, which other, more modern build systems do not share (see meson, I just checked and in under 10 lines of code it lets me run my own substitution script, with the source list, just as it should be). I will not rant against autotools further, suffice to say that verbosity is tradition in there anyway.
Alternatively perhaps you could use the /TP option which causes all files to be treated as C++ regardless of the file name suffix.

In the example above, if I want to only process specific capabilities, I need to know for every platform how it’s being displayed. For an abstraction layer, that’s unexpected. Sorry for the delay in response. I got pulled into working on some other things.

In the 4 step scenario you outlined above, you can use the `--expand` option to add `www.domain.tld` to the certificate under `/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.tld` (make sure `--duplicate` isn't set on the command line or in a Certbot config file).

While this solves the problem of adding a domain to a certificate, it currently isn't possible to remove a name from a lineage (see #2071). Similarly, if the user never requests a cert for `domain.tld` and only requests subdomains, there is currently no way to use `/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.tld` (see #2128).

We certainly want to change this in the future, but currently this isn't possible. The two solutions I see in the meantime are:

1. Submit a PR! These features are in high demand and we will certainly review a PR adding support.
2. After running Certbot, move/fix up the files using another script.

If you decided to use an external script, it would have to:
* Move the directory `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/www.domain.tld`
* Fix the symlinks in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/www.domain.tld`
* Move the directory `/etc/letsencrypt/live/www.domain.tld`
* Fix the paths at the top of `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/www.domain.tld`
* Move the file `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/www.domain.tld.conf`

The problem is that @HenryLeongStat is using Docker Toolbox under windows 7. In this system, a virtualbox mounts `c:/Users` as `/Users`, so the mounting path should be

```
-v /Users/user:/var/lib/whatever
```
instead of
```
-v /C/Users/user:/var/lib/whatever
```
that SoS assumes. Since in theory users can configure virtualbox shared directory with arbitrary pathname, there is very little we can do on the SoS side.

Solution?
1. Allow users to write this mapping into some sos configuration file.
2. Probe virtual box for the path mapping, which is possible with virtualbox command line.

@Vangelis66 to compile it I used [ffmpeg-windows-build-helpers](https://github.com/rdp/ffmpeg-windows-build-helpers) which cross compiles to Windows on linux (I used a VM), to make it work correctly in XP you need to give the `--build-intel-qsv=n` option which disables the Intel QSV and `libmfx`. This might affect performance on more modern CPUs, but as `ffmpeg` is mainly intended for muxing streams which doesn't require any decoding/encoding this shouldn't really be a problem. 

The transcoding option is only an option, and I agree it is a very CPU intensive job. It's not actually targeted at supporting YouTube specifically, as you pointed out a lot of compatible formats are already available from YouTube :) The transcode options were added to give flexibility to the end user and to provide a way to work around issues with some streams. For example, I have also been working on #111 for HLS streams and I have come across a few video streams where the timestamps do not match up with the audio timestamp and this breaks the muxer. They can be fixed by transcoding the video, which forces `ffmpeg` to fix the timestamps. So really it's not something that is meant to be used every time, only when there is a particular need for it :)

Right, but that's just the inherent annoyingness of working with a callback API, right? :-)

If you have something short and synchronous that you want to do, the kind of thing that you'd normally just execute immediately in the Qt thread, except that it has to be done in the Trio thread instead... I think in this case it might actually OK to block the Qt thread while waiting for it. The thread switch adds some bookkeeping overhead, but it's still not going to block the Qt thread for much longer than it would take to execute it normally.

For long-running actions, the only way to do that in Qt/Kivy's native API is to schedule them and then get a callback when they're done – that's just a fundamental property of how Qt/Kivy work, not something that Trio created or can do anything about :-). It'd be nice if we had non-callback-based GUI libraries, but until then I think we just have to live with this.

And in lots of cases, you may be fine just scheduling something to run in the trio thread and then abandon it – for example, `run_sync_soon_in_trio(q.put_nowait, value)`, where `q` is a `trio.hazmat.UnboundedQueue`. This is not the kind of API we like in Trio-land for all the obvious reasons, like, what if the call fails? But in normal operation `UnboundedQueue.put_nowait` never fails; and if something really abnormal goes wrong, and we lose the exception... well, that's the same way that everything works on the Qt side, so we're not really making things worse if there are a few operations in the Qt/Trio interface that have the limitations of Qt.

Once you've created the project you get access to a set of attributes. The `ssh_url_to_repo` attribute provides the URL of the repository. You can use a git library or CLI to clone the project:

```python

subprocess.call(['git', 'clone', git_url])

Yes. Just think of implementing a normal agent. The method which is called
when the env wants an action returns an action vector like in a normal
agent. Now, the MultiAgent, being actually a set of agents, will call each
of its sub-agents for an action given the same state and return the
concatenated action vectors. Your board game will have to ignore the
actions of the agents that are not on turn. That is your board logic then.
For a multiagent situation where every agent can move at the same time
(think Soccer game with different players), this will not require any env
logic.

So I finally have time to look at the problem. Seems to me that this is a missing feature/detail in keras. Specifically `InputLayer` does not export the `input_tensor` parameter (which contains the constant tensor), so it is lost during cloning. The visible effect is that:

1. The `InputLayer.is_placeholder` attr is wrong, and
2. The `Model._feed_inputs` and related attr is the full list of input rather than only what is needed.

Ideally keras should handle this. Now workarounds seems to be not too easy in this case because:

1. The cloning process is dynamic and automatic (the config dict for each object is passed to the class's constructor), so intervention after-the-fact (i.e. after the model has been cloned) would be hard/delicate.
2. I'm not sure if it is possible to override the `get_config` method of `InputLayer`: i) We are trying to export a tensorflow/backend-of-your-choice constant tensor, and ii) Somewhere there is a restriction/check that input layers must use the `InputLayer` class.

The only fallback I can think of now is to manually supply those dummy input values, external to the keras library. But then I would need to read the constant tensor's value, which at least in tensorflow I heard is again not easy (you can only read it in a session, mostly). And this solution is rather ugly IMO.
